3553	  % (pullbackTensorIsoOfLocallyTrivial)⁻¹ ≫ (pullback f).mapIso e ≫ pullbackUnitIso. Depends on
3554	  % the LOCALLY-TRIVIAL comparison lem:pullback_tensor_iso_loctriv and lem:pullback_unit_iso
3555	  % (in hand). The loc-triv hypotheses on M and its inverse N are supplied directly: the
3556	  % consumer carries IsLocallyTrivial, and the inverse N may be taken locally trivial (its dual,
3557	  % via the easy reverse exists_tensorObj_inverse). NO forward bridge IsInvertible ⇒ loc-triv,
3558	  % NO general comparison, NO concrete inverse-image build.
3559	  Let \(f : Y \to X\) be a morphism of schemes and let
3560	  \(M \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) be \(\otimes\)-invertible
3561	  (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}) with a tensor inverse \(N\) and isomorphism
3562	  \(e : M \otimes_X N \cong \mathcal{O}_X\). If both \(M\) and \(N\) are locally trivial
3563	  (\cref{def:IsLocallyTrivial}) --- as holds for every line bundle, and in particular for
3564	  the modules the relative Picard carrier presents --- then the pullback \(f^*M\) is again
3565	  \(\otimes\)-invertible, with inverse \(f^*N\):
3566	  \[
3567	    \mathtt{IsInvertible}(M)
3568	      \;\Longrightarrow\;
3569	    \mathtt{IsInvertible}\bigl((\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.pullback}\,f).\mathrm{obj}\,M\bigr).

3571	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Modules on Ringed Spaces, Lemma
3572	  % \ref{lemma-pullback-invertible} (the pullback of an invertible module is
3573	  % invertible) (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L4142-4147).
3574	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
3575	  % "\begin{lemma}
3576	  % \label{lemma-pullback-invertible}

3578	  % morphism of ringed spaces. The pullback $f^*\mathcal{L}$ of an

3581	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Modules on Ringed Spaces, Lemma
3582	  \texttt{lemma-pullback-invertible}: the pullback \(f^*\mathcal{L}\) of an
3583	  invertible \(\mathcal{O}\)-module along a morphism of ringed spaces is
3584	  invertible.}
3585	\end{lemma}
3586	
3587	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L4149-4157).
3588	% "\begin{proof}
3589	% By Lemma \ref{lemma-invertible}
3590	% there exists an $\mathcal{O}_Y$-module $\mathcal{N}$ such that
3591	% $\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_Y} \mathcal{N} \cong \mathcal{O}_Y$.
3592	% Pulling back we get
3593	% $f^*\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} f^*\mathcal{N} \cong \mathcal{O}_X$
3594	% by Lemma \ref{lemma-tensor-product-pullback}.

3596	% \end{proof}"
3597	\begin{proof}
3598	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible, lem:pullback_tensor_iso_loctriv,
3599	        lem:pullback_unit_iso, def:IsLocallyTrivial}
3600	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Lemma \texttt{lemma-pullback-invertible}.}
3601	  This is the Stacks proof in the project's notation, using the locally-trivial
3602	  comparison \cref{lem:pullback_tensor_iso_loctriv}.
3603	
3604	  By \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}(M)\) (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}) there is a
3605	  witness \(N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) together with an isomorphism
3606	  \(e : M \otimes_X N \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{O}_X\); by hypothesis \(M\) and \(N\)
3607	  are locally trivial (\cref{def:IsLocallyTrivial}). We take the pullback witness
3608	  \((\mathtt{pullback}\,f).\mathrm{obj}\,N = f^*N\) and exhibit \(f^*M\) as
3609	  \(\otimes\)-invertible by the composite
3610	  \[
3611	    (f^*M) \otimes_Y (f^*N)
3612	      \;\xrightarrow[\sim]{\;(\text{\cref{lem:pullback_tensor_iso_loctriv}})^{-1}\;}\;
3613	    f^*(M \otimes_X N)
3614	      \;\xrightarrow[\sim]{\;f^*e\;}\;
3615	    f^*\mathcal{O}_X
3616	      \;\xrightarrow[\sim]{\;\mathtt{pullbackUnitIso}\;}\;
3617	    \mathcal{O}_Y,
3618	  \]
3619	  whose three arrows are, in order: the inverse of the locally-trivial pullback--tensor
3620	  isomorphism \cref{lem:pullback_tensor_iso_loctriv} (applicable because \(M, N\) are
3621	  locally trivial); the image \((\mathtt{pullback}\,f).\mathtt{mapIso}\,e\) of the
3622	  invertibility witness \(e\) under the pullback functor; and the structure-sheaf
3623	  comparison \(\mathtt{pullbackUnitIso}\) identifying \(f^*\mathcal{O}_X\) with
3624	  \(\mathcal{O}_Y\) (\cref{lem:pullback_unit_iso}). Each is an isomorphism --- the first
3625	  by the locally-trivial strong-monoidality of pullback, the second as the image of an
3626	  isomorphism under a functor, the third by \cref{lem:pullback_unit_iso} --- so the
3627	  composite is an isomorphism \(f^*M \otimes_Y f^*N \cong \mathcal{O}_Y\). Hence \(f^*N\)
3628	  is a tensor inverse of \(f^*M\), and \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}(f^*M)\) holds.
3629	\end{proof}

3631	\section{The invertibility-carrier Picard group}
3632	\label{sec:tensorobj_pic_carrier}
3633	
3634	The group law of \cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup} is carried on the
3635	\emph{locally-trivial} predicate \(\mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\), and on that carrier
3636	the existence of a tensor inverse for each class is an extra obligation
3637	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible}): one must \emph{manufacture} the dual
3638	object \(L^{-1} = \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(L, \mathcal{O}_X)\) and prove the
3639	contraction \(L \otimes_X L^{-1} \cong \mathcal{O}_X\), which is the whole content of
3640	the dual-infrastructure build of \cref{sec:tensorobj_dual_infra}.
3641	
3642	This section carries the group law on a \emph{different} predicate for which that
3643	obligation evaporates: the \(\otimes\)-invertibility predicate
3644	\(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\) of \cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible},
3645	\[
3646	  \mathtt{IsInvertible}(M)
3647	  \;:\equiv\;
3648	  \exists\, N,\ \mathrm{Nonempty}\bigl(M \otimes_X N \cong \mathcal{O}_X\bigr).
3649	\]
3650	On this carrier the inverse of a class \([M]\) is supplied by the \emph{membership
3651	witness itself} --- the very \(N\) whose existence \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}(M)\)
3652	asserts --- so no inverse object need be constructed and no separate
3653	tensor-inverse lemma is consumed. The cost is shifted to a routine
3654	well-definedness fact (\cref{lem:isinvertible_inverse_welldef}: the witness \(N\) is
3655	determined up to isomorphism), proved from the monoidal coherence isomorphisms
3656	alone. The carrier is the scheme-theoretic analogue of Mathlib's
3657	\(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\), whose elements are exactly the iso-classes of invertible
3658	modules (Stacks tag 01CX).
3659	
3660	\begin{lemma}
3661	
3662	\leanok
3663	[Associator for $\otimes_X$ on invertible objects (corollary of the unconditional
3664	associator)]
3665	  \label{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso_invertible}
3666	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_assoc_iso_invertible}

1	\chapter{Line-bundle pullback on a relative curve}
2	\label{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/LineBundlePullback.lean
4	
5	% STRATEGY NOTE (iter-173). This chapter is the A.1.b sub-row of Route~A
6	% (positive-genus arm of nonempty_jacobianWitness). It packages the line-bundle
7	% pullback functor \(\pi_T^* : \Pic(T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T)\) along the
8	% projection \(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\) of a relative curve, and the
9	% set-valued relative Picard presheaf
10	% \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) := \Pic(C \times_k T) / \pi_T^*\Pic(T)\)
11	% as a functor on \((\Sch/k)^{op}\). This chapter is gated on
12	% \cref{chap:Picard_RelativeSpec} (iter-172) for the relative-spectrum machinery
13	% that any concrete Lean-side affine-cover argument will need, and it itself
14	% gates A.1.c (\'etale sheafification / \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) as a sheaf), A.2.*
15	% (Quot / Hilbert representability), A.3 (identity component), and A.4
16	% (universal property = Albanese / Jacobian).
17	
18	% NOTE (planner directive iter-173). Per the iter-173 blueprint-review
19	% recommendation (see .archon/task_results/blueprint-reviewer-route173.md,
20	% sub-phase choice), this chapter defines \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) as a
21	% \emph{set-valued} presheaf on \((\Sch/k)^{op}\). The abelian-group refinement
22	% (and the \'etale-sheafification step) belong to the follow-up chapter
23	% Picard_RelPicFunctor.tex (A.1.c). The set-valued formulation lets us discharge
24	% well-definedness of the quotient and functoriality of \(g^*\) as soon as a
25	% line-bundle pullback functor and its composition identity are in hand, with
26	% no reliance on Mathlib's \texttt{AddCommGrp} \'etale-sheafification API which
27	% does not yet exist.
28	
29	\section{Setup and motivation}
30	\label{sec:lbp_setup}
31	
32	Throughout this chapter, fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper geometrically
33	integral curve \(C\) over \(k\) (the curve carrying the Jacobian; the chapter's
34	constructions are functorial in \(C\) and do not formally use these hypotheses
35	until they meet the representability theorems in the downstream A.1.c / A.2.*
36	chapters). A test object is a \(k\)-scheme \(T\); the relative curve over \(T\) is
37	\(C \times_k T \xrightarrow{\pi_T} T\), with \(\pi_C : C \times_k T \to C\) the
38	companion projection. Both \(\pi_T\) and \(\pi_C\) are flat (because base change of
39	flat morphisms is flat and \(C/k\) is flat since \(k\) is a field).
40	
41	The relative Picard presheaf compares two natural line-bundle groups on the
42	product: the Picard group \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) of the total space, and the
43	Picard group \(\Pic(T)\) of the base. The pullback functor \(\pi_T^*\) sends a line
44	bundle on \(T\) to its base-change line bundle on \(C \times_k T\); the
45	\emph{relative Picard presheaf}
46	\[
47	  \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) \;\;:=\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T)\, / \, \pi_T^*\Pic(T)
48	\]
49	is the quotient set (and, with extra structure, the quotient abelian group). It
50	is the universal Set-valued functor on \((\Sch/k)^{op}\) that ``forgets line
51	bundles pulled back from the base'', and is the starting point of the
52	representability theorems of Grothendieck, Mumford, and Kleiman (see
53	\cref{chap:Jacobian} for the wider Route~A picture).
54	
55	\section{Line bundles on a relative curve}
56	\label{sec:lbp_line_bundle}
57	
58	\begin{definition}\leanok
59	[Line bundle on a product]
60	  \label{def:line_bundle_on_product}
61	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.OnProduct}
62	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", §2 (FGA Explained Ch.9 §9.2),
63	  % Definition df:aPf (absolute Picard functor)
64	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1279).
65	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
66	  % "The {\it absolute Picard functor} \(\Pic_X\) is the functor from  the
67	  %  category of (locally Noetherian) \(S\)-schemes \(T\) to the category of
68	  % abelian groups defined by the formula
69	  %     \(\)\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).\(\)"
70	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', §2,
71	  Def.~``absolute Picard functor''
72	  (cf.\ Stacks tag 01HK, Hartshorne II~§6).}
73	  Let \(k\) be a base field, \(C\) a \(k\)-scheme (in our application, a smooth proper
74	  geometrically integral curve), and \(T\) a \(k\)-scheme. A
75	  \emph{line bundle on the relative curve \(C \times_k T\)} is an invertible
76	  \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\)-module, that is, an
77	  \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\)-module \(\mathcal{L}\) such that there exists an
78	  open cover \(\{U_i\}\) of \(C \times_k T\) on which each restriction
79	  \(\mathcal{L}|_{U_i}\) is free of rank one. Two line bundles are isomorphic if
80	  they are isomorphic as \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\)-modules. The set of
81	  isomorphism classes forms an abelian group under tensor product (the
82	  \emph{Picard group}), denoted
83	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\). In Kleiman's notation with \(X = C\) and \(S = \Spec k\),
84	  this is exactly the value
85	  \(\Pic_C(T) = \Pic(C \times_k T)\) of the absolute Picard functor.
86	\end{definition}

127	\section{Project-side IsLocallyTrivial substrate}
128	\label{sec:lbp_isLocallyTrivial_substrate}
129	
130	Mathlib at the pinned commit \texttt{b80f227} ships no
131	\texttt{Scheme.Modules.IsInvertible} predicate (iter-187 \texttt{lean\_local\_search}
132	verdict: empty). The project introduces a stand-in predicate
133	\texttt{Scheme.LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial} encoding rank-one local
134	trivialisation on an affine cover. The subtype
135	\texttt{\{ M : (Limits.pullback πC πT).Modules // IsLocallyTrivial M \}}
136	gives the carrier of \cref{def:line_bundle_on_product}.
137	
138	\begin{definition}
139	
140	\leanok
141	[Local triviality of a \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module]
142	  \label{def:IsLocallyTrivial}
143	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial}
144	  \textit{Source: Stacks~\href{https://stacks.math.columbia.edu/tag/01HH}{01HH}
145	  (locally trivial $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules), specialised to rank one.}
146	  An \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module \(\mathcal{M}\) on a scheme \(X\) is
147	  \emph{locally trivial of rank one} if for every point \(x \in X\) there
148	  exists an affine open \(U \ni x\) and an iso
149	  \(\mathcal{M}|_U \cong \mathcal{O}_U\) (the structure sheaf restricted to
150	  \(U\) viewed as the unit object of \(X.\mathrm{Modules}\)). Formally:
151	  \[
152	    \forall\, x : X, \exists\, U : X.\mathrm{Opens},
153	      x \in U \,\wedge\, \mathtt{IsAffineOpen}\, U \,\wedge\,
154	      \mathtt{Nonempty}\, (\mathcal{M}.\mathrm{restrict}\, U.\iota \cong
155	        \mathtt{SheafOfModules.unit}\, \_).
156	  \]
157	\end{definition}
158	
159	\begin{lemma}
160	
161	\leanok
162	[Pullback preserves local triviality (Stacks 01HH)]
163	  \label{lem:IsLocallyTrivial_pullback}
164	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial.pullback}
165	  \uses{def:IsLocallyTrivial}
166	  \textit{Source: Stacks~\href{https://stacks.math.columbia.edu/tag/01HH}{01HH}
167	  (pullback of a locally trivial module is locally trivial).}
168	  Let \(f : Y \to X\) be a morphism of schemes and let
169	  \(\mathcal{M} : X.\mathrm{Modules}\) be locally trivial of rank one
170	  (\cref{def:IsLocallyTrivial}). Then \(f^*\mathcal{M}\) is locally
171	  trivial of rank one.
172	\end{lemma}
173	\begin{proof}
174	  \uses{def:IsLocallyTrivial}
175	  \leanok
176	  Given \(y : Y\), let \(x = f(y) \in X\). By the local triviality of
177	  \(\mathcal{M}\) at \(x\), there is an affine open \(U \ni x\) and an
178	  iso \(\mathcal{M}|_U \cong \mathcal{O}_U\). The preimage \(f^{-1}U\)
179	  is an open neighbourhood of \(y\), and any sufficiently small affine
180	  open \(V \subseteq f^{-1}U\) containing \(y\) (exists by
181	  \texttt{exists\_isAffineOpen\_mem\_and\_subset}) gives the chart. The
182	  required iso
183	  \((f^*\mathcal{M})|_V \cong \mathcal{O}_V\) follows from the chain:
184	  \texttt{restrictFunctorIsoPullback} (identifying \(\mathrm{restrict}\, V.\iota\)
185	  with \(\mathrm{pullback}\, V.\iota\)), \texttt{pullbackComp} (composing
186	  pullback functors along \(V.\iota \circ f = (g : V \to U) \circ U.\iota\)),
187	  and \texttt{pullbackObjUnitToUnit} (pullback of unit is unit; uses
188	  \(\mathtt{F.Final}\) instance for the underlying continuous functor of an
189	  open immersion).
190	
191	  % NOTE (iter-188 plan-phase): Body lands with 1 narrow named typed sorry
192	  % on the residual chart-iso construction; the affine-chart existence step
193	  % is closed axiom-clean. Closure target iter-188 prover, ~30-50 LOC.
194	\end{proof}
195	
196	\section{The pullback functor along the projection}
197	\label{sec:lbp_pullback_projection}
198	
199	If \(f : Y \to Z\) is a morphism of schemes and \(\mathcal{M}\) is an invertible
200	\(\mathcal{O}_Z\)-module, the pullback \(f^*\mathcal{M}\) is an invertible
201	\(\mathcal{O}_Y\)-module (the pullback of an open cover trivialising \(\mathcal{M}\)
202	trivialises \(f^*\mathcal{M}\), and the pullback of a free module of rank one is
203	free of rank one). This is a standard preservation fact (Stacks tag 01HH); we
204	specialise it to the projection \(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\) and the
205	\(\mathcal{O}_T\)-Picard group.

252	\section{Composition of pullbacks}
253	\label{sec:lbp_compose}
254	
255	\begin{lemma}\leanok
256	[Composition of line-bundle pullbacks]
257	  \label{lem:pullback_compose}
258	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.pullback_pullback_eq}
259	  
260	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", §2 (FGA Explained Ch.9 §9.2),
261	  % Definition df:aPf (the absolute Picard functor as a functor; functoriality
262	  % includes composition)
263	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1279).
264	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
265	  % "The {\it absolute Picard functor} \(\Pic_X\) is the functor from  the
266	  %  category of (locally Noetherian) \(S\)-schemes \(T\) to the category of
267	  % abelian groups defined by the formula
268	  %     \(\)\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).\(\)"
269	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', §2,
270	  Def.~``absolute Picard functor'' --- the word \emph{functor} encodes both the
271	  identity and composition laws (cf.\ Stacks tag 01HG: pullback of an invertible
272	  module is invertible and compatible with composition).}
273	  Let \(C/k\) be as in \cref{def:line_bundle_on_product}, and let
274	  \(g : T' \to T\) be a morphism of \(k\)-schemes. Write
275	  \(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\) and \(\pi_{T'} : C \times_k T' \to T'\) for the
276	  canonical projections, and write
277	  \(g_C := \mathrm{id}_C \times_k g : C \times_k T' \to C \times_k T\) for the
278	  base change of \(g\) along \(C/k\). Then on line bundles we have the equality of
279	  pullback maps
280	  \[
281	    g^* \circ \pi_T^* \;\;=\;\; \pi_{T'}^* \circ g^*
282	    \quad\colon\quad \Pic(T) \;\longrightarrow\; \Pic(C \times_k T'),
283	  \]
284	  and, equivalently, the canonical natural isomorphism of pullback functors
285	  \[
286	    g_C^* \circ \pi_T^* \;\;\cong\;\; \pi_{T'}^* \circ g^*
287	  \]
288	  realises both sides as the pullback along the composite morphism
289	  \(\pi_T \circ g_C = g \circ \pi_{T'} : C \times_k T' \to T\).
290	\end{lemma}
291	
292	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: not applicable --- the statement is one application of
293	% the functoriality of pullback (Kleiman § 2's "absolute Picard functor is a
294	% functor" + Stacks 01HG); no separate proof body is offered in the cited
295	% source, only the structural assertion. The Lean side is therefore a direct
296	% appeal to Mathlib's pullback-of-modules transitivity.
297	\begin{proof}
298	  
299	  
300	\leanok
301	  The square
302	  \[
303	    \begin{array}{ccc}
304	      C \times_k T' & \xrightarrow{g_C} & C \times_k T \\
305	      \;\downarrow\,\pi_{T'} & & \;\downarrow\,\pi_T \\
306	      T' & \xrightarrow{g} & T
307	    \end{array}
308	  \]
309	  is a pullback square (by definition of the fibre product). Equality of the
310	  composites \(\pi_T \circ g_C = g \circ \pi_{T'}\) holds on the nose. Pullback of
311	  \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules is functorial, so for any \(\mathcal{N} \in \Pic(T)\)
312	  there is a canonical isomorphism
313	  \(g_C^*\pi_T^*\mathcal{N} \cong \pi_{T'}^* g^* \mathcal{N}\)
314	  obtained as the composite of pullback-along-composition isomorphisms
315	  \((\pi_T \circ g_C)^*\mathcal{N} \cong (g \circ \pi_{T'})^*\mathcal{N}\)
316	  (Mathlib \texttt{Module.pullback\_comp} on the underlying \(\mathcal{O}\)-module
317	  category, or Stacks tag 01HG for the category-theoretic statement). Passing
318	  to isomorphism classes turns this canonical isomorphism into the equality of
319	  set-valued maps stated above.
320	\end{proof}

322	\section{The relative Picard presheaf (set-valued)}
323	\label{sec:lbp_relpic_quotient}
324	
325	We now package the pullback functor into the relative Picard presheaf. As
326	explained in the planner note at the top of the chapter, we work with the
327	Set-valued version; the group refinement is deferred to A.1.c.
328	
329	\begin{theorem}\leanok
330	[The relative Picard quotient is well defined]
331	  \label{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}
332	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelPicPresheaf.preimage_subgroup}
333	  
334	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", §2 (FGA Explained Ch.9 §9.2),
335	  % Definition df:Pfs (relative Picard functor)
336	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1311-L1318).
337	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
338	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
339	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} \(\Pic_{X/S}\) is defined by
340	  %     \(\)\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).\(\)
341	  %  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
342	  % topologies  by
343	  %     \(\)\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
344	  %     \Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.\(\)
345	  %  \end{dfn}"
346	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', §2,
347	  Def.~``relative Picard functor'' df:Pfs.}
348	  Let \(C/k\) be as in \cref{def:line_bundle_on_product} and let \(T\) be a
349	  \(k\)-scheme. The subset
350	  \[
351	    H_T \;\;:=\;\; \pi_T^* \Pic(T) \;\;\subseteq\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T)
352	  \]
353	  is the image of the pullback map \(\pi_T^*\)
354	  (\cref{def:pullback_along_projection}); in particular it contains the trivial
355	  class and is closed under the multiplication of \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) (so it is
356	  a subgroup). Define
357	  \[
358	    \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) \;\;:=\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \, / \, H_T,
359	  \]
360	  the quotient set whose elements are equivalence classes \([\mathcal{L}]\) of
361	  line bundles \(\mathcal{L} \in \Pic(C \times_k T)\) under the relation
362	  \(\mathcal{L} \sim \mathcal{L}'\) iff
363	  \(\mathcal{L} \otimes \mathcal{L}'^{-1} \in H_T\).
364	  Then \(T \mapsto \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)\) is a well-defined assignment from
365	  \(k\)-schemes to sets, and the natural map
366	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) \to \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)\) is a surjection of sets.
367	\end{theorem}
368	
369	% NOTE (iter-186 review): The iter-186 Lane A.1.b prover landed the body as a
370	% `Setoid` on `OnProduct = (pullback πC πT).Modules` whose relation is
371	% `r L L' := Nonempty (L ≅ L')` (iso-class equivalence; axiom-clean). This
372	% identifies isomorphic modules (giving the underlying set of `Pic(C ×_S T)`
373	% once invertibility is imposed in iter-187+) but does NOT yet quotient by
374	% the pullback subgroup `pi_T^* Pic(T)`. The full quotient requires (a) a
375	% tensor-product structure on `Scheme.Modules` (Mathlib ships
376	% `PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj` but no monoidal structure on
377	% `Scheme.Modules` directly at b80f227), and (b) an inverse for invertible
378	% sheaves (depends on the iter-187+ `IsInvertible` predicate). Iter-187+
379	% refinement: `r L L' := exists N : T.Modules, Nonempty (L tensor L'inv iso
380	% pullbackAlongProjection πC πT N)` once both are available.
381	
382	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: Kleiman §2 does not supply a separate "proof" block for
383	% df:Pfs --- the definition's well-definedness is a direct group-theoretic
384	% observation. The verbatim statement above is the full source content. The
385	% project-side proof is therefore a structural restatement in Lean syntax,
386	% not a translation of an external argument.
387	\begin{proof}
388	  
389	  
390	\leanok
391	  The subset \(H_T = \pi_T^*\Pic(T)\) is a subgroup of \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\)
392	  because the pullback map \(\pi_T^*\) is a homomorphism of abelian groups (its
393	  multiplicativity on tensor products is part of
394	  \cref{def:pullback_along_projection}). Therefore the quotient
395	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) / H_T\) is a well-defined quotient set (equivalently, a
396	  quotient abelian group; we forget the group structure to obtain the
397	  Set-valued version per the planner directive). The surjection
398	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) \to \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)\) is the canonical quotient map
399	  to equivalence classes; surjectivity is by construction.
400	\end{proof}

402	\section{Naturality in the test scheme}
403	\label{sec:lbp_pullback_natural}
404	
405	\begin{theorem}\leanok
406	[Pullback descends through the quotient]
407	  \label{thm:pullback_natural}
408	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelPicPresheaf.functorial}
409	  
410	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", §2 (FGA Explained Ch.9 §9.2),
411	  % Definitions df:aPf + df:Pfs (the relative Picard functor is a functor)
412	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
413	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
414	  % "The {\it absolute Picard functor} \(\Pic_X\) is the functor from  the
415	  %  category of (locally Noetherian) \(S\)-schemes \(T\) to the category of
416	  % abelian groups defined by the formula
417	  %     \(\)\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).\(\)
418	  %  [\dots]
419	  %  \begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
420	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} \(\Pic_{X/S}\) is defined by
421	  %     \(\)\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).\(\)"
422	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', §2,
423	  Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs (the word \emph{functor} encodes naturality in \(T\);
424	  cf.\ Stacks tag 01HG).}
425	  Let \(C/k\) be as in \cref{def:line_bundle_on_product}, and let
426	  \(g : T' \to T\) be a morphism of \(k\)-schemes. The pullback maps
427	  \(g_C^* : \Pic(C \times_k T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T')\) and
428	  \(g^* : \Pic(T) \to \Pic(T')\) make the diagram
429	  \[
430	    \begin{array}{ccc}
431	      \Pic(T) & \xrightarrow{\;\;\pi_T^*\;\;} & \Pic(C \times_k T) \\
432	      \;\downarrow\,g^* & & \;\downarrow\,g_C^* \\
433	      \Pic(T') & \xrightarrow{\;\;\pi_{T'}^*\;\;} & \Pic(C \times_k T')
434	    \end{array}
435	  \]
436	  commute (\cref{lem:pullback_compose}). Therefore \(g_C^*\) sends
437	  \(H_T = \pi_T^*\Pic(T)\) into \(H_{T'} = \pi_{T'}^*\Pic(T')\) and hence induces a
438	  unique set map
439	  \[
440	    g^\sharp \;\;\colon\;\; \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) \;\longrightarrow\;
441	                              \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T'),
442	    \qquad [\mathcal{L}] \;\longmapsto\; [g_C^* \mathcal{L}],
443	  \]
444	  through the quotients of
445	  \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}. The assignment
446	  \(g \mapsto g^\sharp\) satisfies \(\mathrm{id}_T^\sharp = \mathrm{id}\) and
447	  \((g \circ h)^\sharp = g^\sharp \circ h^\sharp\) for any composable
448	  \(h : T'' \to T'\) and \(g : T' \to T\); thus
449	  \(T \mapsto \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)\) extends to a functor
450	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} : (\Sch/k)^{op} \to \mathrm{Set}\).
451	\end{theorem}
452	
453	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: As with df:Pfs, Kleiman §2 does not supply a separate
454	% proof body for the functoriality assertion --- it is part of the definition
455	% (the word "functor" carries the identity and composition laws). The verbatim
456	% quote is therefore the same as the statement.
457	\begin{proof}
458	  
459	  
460	\leanok
461	  Commutativity of the displayed square is the special case
462	  \(g_C^* \circ \pi_T^* = \pi_{T'}^* \circ g^*\) of
463	  \cref{lem:pullback_compose}. Therefore \(g_C^*\) maps the image
464	  \(H_T = \pi_T^*\Pic(T)\) into the image
465	  \(H_{T'} = \pi_{T'}^*\Pic(T')\): an element
466	  \(\pi_T^*\mathcal{N} \in H_T\) with \(\mathcal{N} \in \Pic(T)\) has
467	  \(g_C^*\pi_T^*\mathcal{N} = \pi_{T'}^*g^*\mathcal{N} \in H_{T'}\). Hence
468	  \(g_C^*\) induces a unique map of quotient sets
469	  \(g^\sharp : \Pic(C \times_k T)/H_T \to \Pic(C \times_k T')/H_{T'}\) by the
470	  universal property of the quotient set
471	  (\cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}). The identity law
472	  \(\mathrm{id}^\sharp = \mathrm{id}\) is immediate from
473	  \(\mathrm{id}^* = \mathrm{id}\) for pullback of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules. The
474	  composition law \((g \circ h)^\sharp = g^\sharp \circ h^\sharp\) follows from
475	  the corresponding statement for \(\mathcal{O}\)-module pullback
476	  \((g \circ h)^* = h^* \circ g^*\) (passing through the quotient is functorial).
477	  Hence \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} : (\Sch/k)^{op} \to \mathrm{Set}\) is a presheaf.
478	\end{proof}

34	\section{Setup and motivation}
35	\label{sec:relpic_setup}
36	
37	Fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper geometrically integral curve \(C\) over
38	\(k\). As in \cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}, write
39	\(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\) for the projection of a relative curve over a
40	\(k\)-scheme \(T\) and
41	\(\pi_T^* : \Pic(T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T)\) for the induced pullback of
42	isomorphism classes of line bundles (\cref{def:pullback_along_projection}).
43	
44	The set-valued relative Picard presheaf
45	\[
46	  \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) \;\;=\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \, / \, \pi_T^*\Pic(T)
47	\]
48	of \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined} was set up forgetting the
49	abelian-group structure inherited from tensor product. This chapter restores
50	that structure and then sheafifies. The first half (\S~REF
51	and \S~REF) verifies that the quotient is canonically
52	an abelian group and the functoriality of \cref{thm:pullback_natural} respects
53	that group structure; the result is a group-valued presheaf
54	\(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} : (\Sch/k)^{op} \to \Ab\) on the category of \(k\)-schemes. The
55	second half (\S~REF) takes the \'etale sheafification
56	\(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et} := (\Pic^\sharp_{C/k})^\sim\) in the \'etale topology on
57	\((\Sch/k)^{op}\), and verifies that sheafification preserves the abelian-group
58	target. The \'etale-sheafified functor is the object represented by the Picard
59	scheme in \cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability}.
60	
61	\section{Group structure on the relative Picard quotient}
62	\label{sec:relpic_group}
63	
64	The Picard group \(\Pic(X)\) of any scheme is canonically an abelian group under
65	tensor product of line bundles (Stacks tag 01CR; the inverse of \([\mathcal{L}]\)
66	is $[\mathcal{L}^{-1}] = [\mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{L},
67	\mathcal{O}_X)]$). The pullback map
68	\(\pi_T^* : \Pic(T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T)\) of
69	\cref{def:pullback_along_projection} respects this structure: pullback of
70	\(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules sends \(\mathcal{O}_T\) to \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\)
71	and is multiplicative on tensor products.
72	
73	\begin{lemma}\leanok
74	[Group structure descends to the relative Picard quotient]
75	  \label{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}
76	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.addCommGroup}
77	  
78	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
79	  % Definition df:aPf + Definition df:Pfs (the absolute and relative Picard
80	  % functors are \emph{abelian-group}-valued, by construction; the relative
81	  % Picard functor is defined as a quotient \emph{group})
82	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
83	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
84	  % "The {\it absolute Picard functor} \(\Pic_X\) is the functor from  the
85	  %  category of (locally Noetherian) \(S\)-schemes \(T\) to the category of
86	  % abelian groups defined by the formula
87	  %     \(\)\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).\(\)
88	  %  [\dots]
89	  %  \begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
90	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} \(\Pic_{X/S}\) is defined by
91	  %     \(\)\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).\(\)"
92	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2,
93	  Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs (the Picard functors are abelian-group-valued, and the
94	  relative one is a quotient of abelian groups; cf.\ Stacks tag 01CR for the
95	  abelian-group structure on \(\Pic\)).}
96	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve and let \(T\) be a
97	  \(k\)-scheme. The Picard group \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is an abelian group under
98	  tensor product of line bundles, and the pullback map
99	  \[
100	    \pi_T^* \;\colon\; \Pic(T) \;\longrightarrow\; \Pic(C \times_k T)
101	  \]
102	  of \cref{def:pullback_along_projection} is a group homomorphism. Its image
103	  \[
104	    H_T \;\;:=\;\; \pi_T^* \Pic(T)
105	  \]
106	  is therefore a subgroup of \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\), and the quotient set of
107	  \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}
108	  \[
109	    \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) \;\;=\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \, / \, H_T
110	  \]
111	  inherits a canonical abelian-group structure under which the quotient map
112	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) \twoheadrightarrow \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)\) is a surjective
113	  homomorphism with kernel exactly \(H_T\).
114	\end{lemma}
115	
116	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: Kleiman \S 2 does not supply a separate "proof" block
117	% for df:Pfs; the abelian-group structure is part of the definition (the word
118	% \emph{group} appears in df:aPf already, and df:Pfs is a quotient of the same
119	% abelian groups). The verbatim quotes for the statements above are therefore
120	% the same as for the lemma. The project-side proof is a structural restatement.
121	\begin{proof}
122	  
123	  The abelian-group structure on \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) under tensor product is
124	  standard (Stacks tag 01CR or Hartshorne II~\S 6): for line bundles
125	  \(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{L}'\), the tensor product
126	  \(\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}} \mathcal{L}'\) is again
127	  invertible, the structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\) is the unit, and
128	  the dual \(\mathcal{H}om(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T})\) is the
129	  inverse. Pullback of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules sends
130	  \(\mathcal{O}_T\) to \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\) (the structure sheaf is
131	  preserved by inverse image) and satisfies
132	  $\pi_T^*(\mathcal{N} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_T} \mathcal{N}') \cong
133	   \pi_T^*\mathcal{N} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}} \pi_T^*\mathcal{N}'$
134	  by the standard tensor compatibility of pullback. Therefore \(\pi_T^*\)
135	  preserves the unit, sends inverses to inverses, and is multiplicative on
136	  tensor products; it is a homomorphism of abelian groups.
137	
138	  The image \(H_T = \pi_T^*\Pic(T)\) is then a subgroup. The quotient set
139	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) / H_T\) of
140	  \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined} carries the canonical quotient
141	  abelian-group structure (Lagrange's theorem on cosets specialised to an
142	  abelian quotient): for \(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{L}' \in \Pic(C \times_k T)\), set
143	  \([\mathcal{L}] + [\mathcal{L}'] := [\mathcal{L} \otimes \mathcal{L}']\);
144	  well-definedness follows from the subgroup condition. The canonical map
145	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T) / H_T\) is by construction a
146	  surjective group homomorphism with kernel exactly \(H_T\).
147	\end{proof}

34	\section{Setup and motivation}
35	\label{sec:fga_pic_setup}
36	
37	Throughout this chapter, fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper geometrically
38	integral curve \(C\) over \(k\) (the curve carrying the Jacobian). The relative
39	Picard presheaf \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} : (\Sch/k)^{op} \to \Set\) of
40	\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback} has already been packaged --- as a
41	set-valued functor at A.1.b, and refined to its abelian-group-valued
42	\'etale-sheafified version \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) at
43	\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} (A.1.c). The Quot scheme machinery of
44	\cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme} (A.2.b) supplies the representing engine: for
45	\(C/k\) projective and flat with integral geometric fibers, the Hilbert scheme
46	\(\Hilb_{C/k}\) and its open subscheme \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k}\) of relative effective
47	divisors are both representable by quasi-projective \(k\)-schemes. Grothendieck's
48	existence theorem for \(\Pic\) assembles these two ingredients via the Abel
49	map \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k} \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) to produce a representing scheme,
50	the \emph{Picard scheme} \(\Pic_{C/k}\). The present chapter packages this
51	assembly: a line-bundle / Quot correspondence, the FGA representability
52	theorem, and the abelian-group-scheme refinement.
53	
54	% SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 4 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.4);
55	% arXiv:math/0504020, p.~26. Read from
56	% references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex.

58	\section{Line bundles and the Quot / Hilbert scheme}
59	\label{sec:fga_pic_line_bundle_quot}

61	The bridge from line bundles on \(C \times_k T\) to the Quot scheme is the
62	Abel map: a relative effective divisor \(D \subset C \times_k T\) over \(T\)
63	(an \(S\)-flat closed subscheme whose ideal is invertible) determines a closed
64	subscheme of \(C \times_k T\) flat over \(T\), hence a \(T\)-point of the Hilbert
65	functor \(\Hilb_{C/k}(T)\), which is the special case \(\Quot_{\mathcal{O}_C/C/k}\)
66	of the Quot functor of \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}; and it determines an
67	invertible sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}(D)\) on the relative curve,
68	representing a class in \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}(T)\).

106	  by an open subscheme \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k} \subseteq \Hilb_{C/k}\) of the Hilbert scheme
107	  of \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme} (with \(\Hilb_{C/k}\) the special case
108	  \(\Quot_{\mathcal{O}_C/C/k}\) of the Quot functor). The Abel map is therefore
109	  representable by a morphism of \(k\)-schemes $A_{C/k} : \mathrm{Div}_{C/k} \to
110	  \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}$.
111	\end{lemma}
112	
113	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: (from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex,
114	% L1843-L1866, proof of th:repDiv)
115	% "Set \(H:=\IHilb_{X/S}\), and let \(W\subset X\x H\) be the universal
116	% (closed) subscheme, and \(q\:W\to H\) the projection.  Let \(V\) denote the
117	% set of points \(w\in W\) at which \(W\) is an effective divisor. Plainly \(V\)
118	% is open in \(W\).  Set \(Z:=q(W-V)\).  Then \(Z\) is closed because \(q\) is
119	% proper.  Set \(U:=H-Z\).  Then \(U\) is open, and \(q^{-1}U\) is an effective
120	% divisor in \(X\x U\).  In fact, since \(q\) is flat, \(q^{-1}U\) is a relative
121	% effective divisor in \(X\x U/U\).
122	% [\dots]"
123	\begin{proof}
124	  
125	  By \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}, the Hilbert functor $\Hilb_{C/k} =
126	  \Quot_{\mathcal{O}_C/C/k}\( is representable by a \)k$-scheme; this uses
127	  \(C/k\) projective and flat (the smooth proper curve hypothesis is
128	  stronger). Let \(W \subseteq C \times_k \Hilb_{C/k}\) be the universal
129	  closed subscheme and \(q : W \to \Hilb_{C/k}\) the projection. The locus
130	  \(V \subseteq W\) where \(W\) is locally an effective Cartier divisor (i.e.\
131	  cut out by one regular element) is open. The complement \(W \setminus V\)
132	  is closed in \(W\), and the image $Z := q(W \setminus V) \subseteq
133	  \Hilb_{C/k}\( is closed because \)q\( is proper. Hence \)\mathrm{Div}_{C/k} :=
134	  \Hilb_{C/k} \setminus Z\( is open in \)\Hilb_{C/k}$ and represents the
135	  subfunctor of relative effective divisors: on \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k}\) the
136	  restriction of \(W\) is flat and cut out by one regular element fiberwise,
137	  so by the standard relative-divisor criterion (Kleiman~\S 3
138	  Lem.~lm:ctn) it is a relative effective divisor on $C \times_k
139	  \mathrm{Div}_{C/k} / \mathrm{Div}_{C/k}$.
140	
141	  The Abel map sends a relative effective divisor \(D \subseteq C \times_k T\)
142	  to the invertible sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}(D) := \mathcal{I}_D^{-1}\)
143	  (the dual of the ideal sheaf, which is invertible by the divisor
144	  hypothesis). The assignment is functorial in \(T\): pullback of an ideal
145	  sheaf along \(\mathrm{id}_C \times_k g : C \times_k T' \to C \times_k T\)
146	  takes the ideal of \(D\) to the ideal of \(D_{T'}\) (since \(D\) is flat over
147	  \(T\), the schematic inverse image \(D_{T'}\) is a relative effective
148	  divisor on \(C \times_k T'/T'\), again by Kleiman~\S 3 Lem.~lm:ctn), hence
149	  takes \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}(D)\) to \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T'}(D_{T'})\).

57	
58	\section{Setup and statement}
59	\label{sec:albaneseup_setup}
60	
61	Fix an algebraically closed field \(\bar k\) and a smooth proper geometrically
62	irreducible curve \(C\) over \(\bar k\) of genus \(g = g(C) > 0\) (the positive-genus
63	hypothesis is essential: it is precisely the standing hypothesis of Milne's
64	\S III.6). Fix moreover a \(\bar k\)-rational point \(P_0 \in C(\bar k)\) to serve
65	as the basepoint of the Albanese construction.
66	
67	By the A.3 row of Route~A (the identity-component construction applied to the
68	representable Picard scheme of
69	\cref{def:pic_scheme} in \cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability}) the
70	identity component \(\Pic^0_{C/k} \subseteq \Pic_{C/k}\) is a smooth proper
71	geometrically irreducible \(\bar k\)-group scheme of dimension \(g\); we write
72	\(J := \Pic^0_{C/k}\) and let \(\eta_J \colon \Spec \bar k \to J\) denote its
73	identity section, in the project's convention the chosen \(\mathbf 1\)-pointed
74	morphism that represents the unit element of the underlying group object on
75	\(J\).
76	
77	The universal \(T\)-points of \(J\) are degree-\(0\) line bundles on $C \times_{\bar k}
78	T\(, taken modulo line bundles pulled back from \)T$ (\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback};
79	the identity component then refines this to the degree-zero subfunctor on the
80	\'etale sheafification). This moduli interpretation is what underlies the
81	Abel--Jacobi morphism \(\iota_{P_0} \colon C \to J\) of
82	\cref{lem:abel_jacobi_morphism}. The universal homomorphism $\psi \colon J
83	\to A\( corresponding to a pointed morphism \)\varphi \colon C \to A$ is
84	constructed by Milne's symmetric-power route: the symmetric assignment
85	\((P_1, \ldots, P_g) \mapsto \varphi(P_1) + \cdots + \varphi(P_g)\) defines a
86	regular morphism \(\widetilde \psi \colon \mathrm{Sym}^g C \to A\), the
87	birational morphism \(\mathrm{Sym}^g C \dashrightarrow J\) inverts to a
88	rational map \(J \dashrightarrow A\), and the rational-map extension theorem
89	\cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends} promotes that rational map to the
90	desired regular morphism \(\psi\).
91	
92	The goal of the chapter is to establish:
93	
94	\begin{theorem}
95	
96	\leanok
97	[Albanese universal property of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) -- Milne III~\S 6, Proposition~6.1]
98	  \label{thm:albanese_universal_property}
99	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Pic0.albanese_universal_property}
100	  
101	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Proposition~6.1, \S III.6, doc p.~104
102	  % (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 110).
103	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
104	  % "PROPOSITION 6.1. Let \(P\) be a \(k\)-rational point on \(C\).  The map
105	  %  \(f^P\colon C\,\to\,J\) has the following universal property: for any
106	  %  map \(\varphi\colon C\to A\) from \(C\) into an abelian variety sending
107	  %  \(P\) to \(0\), there is a unique homomorphism \(\psi\colon J\to A\) such
108	  %  that \(\varphi=\psi\circ f^P\)."
109	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Proposition~6.1, \S III.6, p.~104.}
110	
111	  Let \(C\) be a complete nonsingular curve of genus \(g > 0\) over an algebraically
112	  closed field \(\bar k\), let \(P_0 \in C(\bar k)\) be a \(\bar k\)-rational point,
113	  and write \(J := \Pic^0_{C/k}\), \(\iota_{P_0} \colon C \to J\) for the
114	  Abel--Jacobi morphism of \cref{lem:abel_jacobi_morphism}. Then for every
115	  abelian variety \(A\) over \(\bar k\) and every pointed morphism
116	  \(\varphi \colon C \to A\) with \(\varphi(P_0) = \eta_A\), there exists a unique
117	  homomorphism of group schemes \(\psi \colon J \to A\) such that
118	  \(\varphi = \psi \circ \iota_{P_0}\).
119	\end{theorem}
120	
121	The rest of this chapter develops the sub-lemmas
122	(\cref{lem:abel_jacobi_morphism}, \cref{def:symmetric_power_curve},
123	\cref{lem:symmetric_product_av_map}, \cref{lem:symmetric_product_to_jacobian},
124	and \cref{lem:descent_through_birational_sigma}) that the proof rests on,
125	then gives the proof.
126	
127	\section{The Abel--Jacobi morphism at a marked point}
128	\label{sec:albaneseup_abel_jacobi}
129	
130	The first sub-lemma packages the construction and the elementary regularity of
131	the Abel--Jacobi morphism \(\iota_{P_0}\) at the basepoint \(P_0\). It is the
132	content gated on A.3 (existence of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) as a representable group
133	scheme).
134	
135	\begin{lemma}\leanok
136	[Abel--Jacobi morphism at a basepoint]
137	  \label{lem:abel_jacobi_morphism}
138	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Pic0.abelJacobi}
139	  
140	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], \S III.6, the canonical map
141	  % \(f^P\colon C\to J\) (introduction to Proposition~6.1), doc p.~104
142	  % (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 110).
143	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
144	  % "PROPOSITION 6.1. Let \(P\) be a \(k\)-rational point on \(C\).  The map
145	  %  \(f^P\colon C\to J\) has the following universal property: for any map
146	  %  \(\varphi\colon C\to A\) from \(C\) into an abelian variety sending \(P\)
147	  %  to \(0\), there is a unique homomorphism \(\psi\colon J\to A\) such that

0	\chapter{Flat base change for the pushforward of a quasi-coherent sheaf ($i=0$)}
1	\label{chap:Cohomology_FlatBaseChange}
2	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Section "Cohomology and
3	% base change, I", and Tag 02KH (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex and
4	% references/stacks-coherent.md).
5	
6	\section{Setup and motivation}
7	
8	This chapter establishes the $i=0$ (i.e.\ \(H^0\) / direct-image) case of flat
9	base change: the formation of the pushforward \(f_*\mathcal{F}\) of a
10	quasi-coherent sheaf commutes with flat base change on the target. It is the
11	foundational, fully self-contained slice of the cohomology-and-base-change
12	package that ultimately underlies relative representability statements; the
13	higher derived cases \(R^i f_*\) for \(i \geq 1\) are treated separately and are
14	out of scope here.
15	
16	Throughout, \(f : X \to S\) is a morphism of schemes that is quasi-compact and
17	quasi-separated, and \(\mathcal{F}\) is a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module.
18	A base change of \(f\) along an arbitrary morphism \(g : S' \to S\) is recorded
19	by the cartesian square obtained from the fibre product
20	\(X' = X \times_S S'\):
21	\[
22	  \begin{array}{ccc}
23	    X' & \xrightarrow{\;g'\;} & X \\[2pt]
24	    {\scriptstyle f'}\big\downarrow & & \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle f} \\[2pt]
25	    S' & \xrightarrow{\;g\;} & S
26	  \end{array}
27	\]
28	Here \(f' : X' \to S'\) is the base change of \(f\), and \(g' : X' \to X\) is the
29	first projection. We write \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\) for the pullback
30	of \(\mathcal{F}\) to \(X'\).
31	
32	\section{The canonical base-change map}
33	
34	\begin{definition}\leanok
35	[Base-change map for the pushforward]
36	  \label{def:pushforward_base_change_map}
37	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushforwardBaseChangeMap}
38	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Section "Cohomology and
39	  % base change, I", base-change diagram (\ref{equation-base-change-diagram})
40	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L877--904).
41	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $f : X \to S$ be a morphism of schemes.
42	  % Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent sheaf on $X$.
43	  % Suppose further that $g : S' \to S$ is any morphism of schemes. Denote
44	  % $X' = X_{S'} = S' \times_S X$ the base change of $X$ and denote
45	  % $f' : X' \to S'$ the base change of $f$.
46	  % Also write $g' : X' \to X$ the projection,
47	  % and set $\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}$."
48	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, \S\,Cohomology and base
49	  change, I.}
50	  Let \(f : X \to S\) be a morphism of schemes, \(\mathcal{F}\) a quasi-coherent
51	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module, and \(g : S' \to S\) an arbitrary morphism, giving the
52	  cartesian square above with projection \(g' : X' \to X\) and
53	  \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\). The \emph{base-change map for the
54	  pushforward} is the canonical morphism of \(\mathcal{O}_{S'}\)-modules
55	  \[
56	    g^*\bigl(f_*\mathcal{F}\bigr) \longrightarrow f'_*\bigl((g')^*\mathcal{F}\bigr)
57	    = f'_*\mathcal{F}' .
58	  \]
59	  It is adjoint to the morphism
60	  \(f_*\mathcal{F} \to g_*\, f'_*\mathcal{F}' = f_*\,(g')_*\mathcal{F}'\) obtained
61	  by applying \(f_*\) to the unit
62	  \(\mathcal{F} \to (g')_*(g')^*\mathcal{F}\) of the
63	  \(((g')^*, (g')_*)\)-adjunction, using the commutativity
64	  \(g \circ f' = f \circ g'\) of the square.
65	\end{definition}
66	
67	\section{The affine case}
68	
69	\subsection{Locality of isomorphisms for \texttt{Scheme.Modules} morphisms}
70	
71	Before treating the affine computation we record three project-local supplements
72	to Mathlib. Mathlib provides the per-open criterion
73	(\(\mathrm{IsIso}\,\varphi \iff \forall U,\ \mathrm{IsIso}(\varphi_U)\)) and a
74	stalkwise isomorphism criterion for \(\mathrm{TopCat.Sheaf}\)-valued morphisms, but
75	it does not package the stalk-local or basis-local criterion at the level of
76	morphisms of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. The following lemmas bridge that gap; they
77	are exactly the locality tools used in the first reduction of the affine
78	base-change lemma below, where one checks the base-change map after restricting to
79	the affine opens of the base. As bespoke infrastructure they carry no external
80	citation and stand on their proof sketches.
81	
82	\begin{lemma}\leanok
83	[Stalk-local criterion for isomorphisms of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules]
84	  \label{lem:modules_isIso_iff_stalk}
85	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.isIso_iff_isIso_stalkFunctor_map}
86	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\varphi : M \to N\) a morphism of sheaves of
87	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. Then \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism if and only if the
88	  underlying morphism of abelian-group presheaves induces an isomorphism on the
89	  stalk at every point \(x \in X\).
90	\end{lemma}
91	
92	\begin{proof}
93	
94	
95	\leanok
96	  If \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism, then so is its image under the forgetful
97	  functor to abelian-group presheaves, and the stalk functor at each \(x\),
98	  being a functor, carries isomorphisms to isomorphisms. Conversely, assume the
99	  stalk map is an isomorphism at every \(x\). Package the underlying abelian-group
100	  presheaves of \(M\) and \(N\) as sheaves of abelian groups, and \(\varphi\) as a
101	  morphism of these sheaves; the stalkwise-isomorphism hypothesis lets us apply the
102	  \(\mathrm{TopCat.Sheaf}\)-level stalk-local isomorphism criterion to conclude that
103	  this morphism of sheaves of abelian groups is an isomorphism. Pushing it through
104	  the forgetful functor to presheaves yields an isomorphism of the underlying
105	  abelian-group presheaf morphisms, and since the forgetful functor from
106	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules to abelian-group presheaves reflects isomorphisms,
107	  \(\varphi\) itself is an isomorphism.
108	\end{proof}
109	
110	\begin{lemma}\leanok
111	[Basis-local criterion for isomorphisms of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules]
112	  \label{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis}
113	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.isIso_of_isIso_app_of_isBasis}
114	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_iff_stalk}
115	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, let \(\{B_i\}_{i \in \iota}\) be a family of opens whose
116	  range is a basis of the topology of \(X\), and let \(\varphi : M \to N\) be a
117	  morphism of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. If \(\varphi\) restricts to an
118	  isomorphism on the sections over every basic open \(B_i\), then \(\varphi\) is an
119	  isomorphism.
120	\end{lemma}
121	
122	\begin{proof}
123	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_iff_stalk}
124	  \leanok
125	  By Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_iff_stalk} it suffices to prove that the induced
126	  stalk map is bijective at each point \(x \in X\). For injectivity, a germ that
127	  vanishes at \(x\) already vanishes on some open neighbourhood, which may be
128	  shrunk to a basic open \(B_i\); on \(B_i\) the section map \(\varphi_{B_i}\) is
129	  injective, so the original section is zero, giving injectivity of the stalk map
130	  from injectivity over the basis. For surjectivity, an arbitrary germ at \(x\) is
131	  represented by a section of \(N\) over some basic open \(B_i\) containing \(x\);
132	  since \(\varphi_{B_i}\) is surjective, this section lifts to a section of \(M\)
133	  over \(B_i\), whose germ at \(x\) maps to the given germ. Thus the stalk map is
134	  bijective, and \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism.
135	\end{proof}
136	
137	\begin{lemma}\leanok
138	[Affine-open locality criterion for isomorphisms of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules]
139	  \label{lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens}
140	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.isIso_iff_isIso_app_affineOpens}
141	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis}
142	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\varphi : M \to N\) a morphism of sheaves of
143	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. Then \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism if and only if it
144	  restricts to an isomorphism on the sections over every affine open of \(X\).
145	\end{lemma}
146	
147	\begin{proof}
148	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis}
149	  \leanok

0	\chapter{Higher direct images $R^i f_*$ of quasi-coherent sheaves ($i \geq 1$)}
1	\label{chap:Cohomology_HigherDirectImage}
2	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/HigherDirectImage.lean
3	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Tags 02KE
4	% (Quasi-coherence of higher direct images), 02KG (relative affine vanishing),
5	% and 02KH (Flat base change) (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex and
6	% references/stacks-coherent.md).
7	
8	\section{Setup and motivation}
9	
10	This chapter treats the higher derived direct images \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) for
11	\(i \geq 1\), complementing the \(i = 0\) (direct-image) case of
12	Chapter~\ref{chap:Cohomology_FlatBaseChange}. These higher direct images are the
13	single deepest foundation of the cohomology-and-base-change package: the
14	Cohen--Macaulay regularity bound, semicontinuity of fibre cohomology, and the
15	\(m\)-regularity estimate of FGA all rest on the basic finiteness and base-change
16	properties of \(R^i f_*\) recorded here.
17	
18	We use the classical cohomology-sheaf formulation (not the derived category) and a
19	{\v C}ech-complex approach, parallel to the \(i = 0\) treatment of
20	Chapter~\ref{chap:Cohomology_FlatBaseChange}.
21	
22	Throughout, \(f : X \to S\) is a morphism of schemes that is quasi-compact and
23	quasi-separated, and \(\mathcal{F}\) is a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module.
24	A base change of \(f\) along a morphism \(g : S' \to S\) is recorded by the
25	cartesian square obtained from the fibre product \(X' = X \times_S S'\):
26	\[
27	  \begin{array}{ccc}
28	    X' & \xrightarrow{\;g'\;} & X \\[2pt]
29	    {\scriptstyle f'}\big\downarrow & & \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle f} \\[2pt]
30	    S' & \xrightarrow{\;g\;} & S
31	  \end{array}
32	\]
33	Here \(f' : X' \to S'\) is the base change of \(f\), \(g' : X' \to X\) is the first
34	projection, and \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\) is the pullback of
35	\(\mathcal{F}\) to \(X'\).
36	
37	\section{Definition of the higher direct images}
38	
39	\begin{definition}\leanok
40	[Higher direct image]
41	  \label{def:higher_direct_image}
42	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.higherDirectImage}
43	  % NOTE (iter-233): the Lean `higherDirectImage` carries an explicit instance
44	  % hypothesis `[HasInjectiveResolutions X.Modules]`, because Mathlib has

60	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, \S\,Quasi-coherence of
61	  higher direct images (and the underlying Cohomology chapter).}
62	  Let \(f : X \to S\) be a morphism of schemes and let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a
63	  quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module. For \(i \geq 0\) the \emph{\(i\)-th
64	  higher direct image} \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) is the \(i\)-th right derived functor
65	  of the pushforward functor \(f_*\) applied to \(\mathcal{F}\). Concretely,
66	  \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) is the \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-module obtained as the
67	  sheafification of the presheaf
68	  \[
69	    V \;\longmapsto\; H^i\bigl(f^{-1}(V),\, \mathcal{F}|_{f^{-1}(V)}\bigr),
70	    \qquad V \subset S \text{ open},
71	  \]
72	  where \(H^i(-, -)\) denotes sheaf cohomology. For \(i = 0\) this recovers the
73	  ordinary pushforward \(R^0 f_*\mathcal{F} = f_*\mathcal{F}\).
74	\end{definition}
